mission.title: Install and Configure Tokenetes on Kubernetes
mission.description: Tokenetes is an open source Transaction Tokens (TraTs) Service — an implementation of the IETF OAuth Transaction Tokens draft specification. It enables secure propagation of identity and context across microservices via transaction tokens. Tokenetes uses SPIRE for workload identity and requires tconfigd for managing TraT configurations. A complete installation involves three phases: tconfigd, the Tokenetes service, and Tokenetes agents (sidecars for TraT verification).

steps.title: Set up SPIRE for Tokenetes
steps.description:
SPIRE must be running in your Kubernetes cluster before installing Tokenetes components. Configure the SPIRE server with the k8sbundle notifier plugin and ensure the SPIRE ClusterRole has the required permissions.

**1. Add the k8sbundle notifier to your SPIRE server configuration:**
```yaml
Notifier "k8sbundle" {
    plugin_data {
        webhook_label = "tokenetes.io/webhook"
    }
}
```

**2. Ensure the SPIRE server ClusterRole includes webhook permissions:**
```yaml
- apiGroups: ["admissionregistration.k8s.io"]
  resources: ["mutatingwebhookconfigurations", "validatingwebhookconfigurations"]
  verbs: ["get", "list", "patch", "watch"]
```

**3. Register tconfigd with SPIRE:**
```bash
kubectl exec -n spire spire-server-0 -- \
    /opt/spire/bin/spire-server entry create \
    --dns tconfigd.tokenetes-system.svc \
    -spiffeID spiffe://[your-trust-domain]/tconfigd \
    -parentID spiffe://[your-trust-domain]/ns/spire/sa/spire-agent \
    -selector k8s:ns:tokenetes-system \
    -selector k8s:sa:tconfigd-service-account
```

**4. Register the Tokenetes service with SPIRE:**
```bash
kubectl exec -n spire spire-server-0 -- \
    /opt/spire/bin/spire-server entry create \
    -spiffeID spiffe://[your-trust-domain]/tokenetes \
    -parentID spiffe://[your-trust-domain]/ns/spire/sa/spire-agent \
    -selector k8s:ns:[your-namespace] \
    -selector k8s:sa:tokenetes-service-account
```

**5. Register each microservice that will verify TraTs:**
```bash
kubectl exec -n spire spire-server-0 -- \
    /opt/spire/bin/spire-server entry create \
    -spiffeID spiffe://[your-trust-domain]/[service-name] \
    -parentID spiffe://[your-trust-domain]/ns/spire/sa/spire-agent \
    -selector k8s:ns:[your-namespace] \
    -selector k8s:sa:[service-account-name]
```
Repeat for each microservice in your application.

steps.title: Install tconfigd
steps.description:
tconfigd manages Transaction Token (TraT) configurations as Kubernetes custom resources. It must be running before deploying the Tokenetes service.

**1. Clone and navigate to the installation directory:**
```bash
git clone https://github.com/tokenetes/tconfigd.git
cd tconfigd/installation
```

**2. Edit `config.yaml` with your environment settings:**
```yaml
enableTratInterception: "true"          # "true" for interception mode, "false" for delegation mode
spireAgentHostDir: "/run/spire/sockets" # Path to SPIRE agent socket directory
tokenetesSpiffeId: "spiffe://[your-trust-domain]/tokenetes"  # SPIFFE ID of the Tokenetes service
agentApiPort: "9030"                    # Agent API port (used in delegation mode)
agentInterceptorPort: "9050"            # Agent interceptor port (used in interception mode)
```

**3. Run the install script:**
```bash
./install.sh
```

**4. Verify tconfigd is running:**
```bash
kubectl get pods -n tokenetes-system
```
You should see the tconfigd pod in `Running` state.

steps.title: Clone the Tokenetes repository
steps.description:
Clone the Tokenetes repository which contains the Kubernetes manifests:
```bash
git clone https://github.com/tokenetes/tokenetes.git
cd tokenetes
```

steps.title: Configure the Tokenetes manifests
steps.description:
Before deploying, update the Kubernetes manifest files in the `kubernetes/` directory with your environment-specific values:

1. Replace `[your-namespace]` with your target namespace in all YAML files
2. Replace `[your-trust-domain]` with your SPIRE trust domain
3. Update the SPIRE agent socket path in `tokenetes-deployment.yaml` if your SPIRE agent socket is not at the default location (`/run/spire/sockets`)

```bash
# Create the target namespace
kubectl create namespace <your-namespace>
```

steps.title: Deploy the Tokenetes service
steps.description:
Apply the Kubernetes manifests from the `kubernetes/` directory in order:
```bash
kubectl apply -f kubernetes/tokenetes-service-account.yaml
kubectl apply -f kubernetes/tokenetes-deployment.yaml
kubectl apply -f kubernetes/tokenetes-service.yaml
```
Note: The Tokenetes service is deployed per application namespace, not cluster-wide. Repeat for each namespace that needs TraT support.

steps.title: Set up Tokenetes Agents
steps.description:
Tokenetes agents are sidecar containers injected into your application pods to handle TraT verification. tconfigd's mutating webhook automatically injects the agent sidecar when properly annotated.

**1. Label your application namespace to enable agent injection:**
```bash
kubectl label namespace <your-namespace> tokenetes-enabled=true
```

**2. Add annotations to your application deployments:**
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: <your-service>
  namespace: <your-namespace>
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        tokenetes/inject-sidecar: "true"        # Enable agent sidecar injection
        tokenetes/agent-mode: "interception"     # "interception" or "delegation"
        tokenetes/service-port: "<port-number>"  # Required for interception mode
```

**3. Restart your deployments to trigger injection:**
```bash
kubectl rollout restart deployment <your-service> -n <your-namespace>
```

**Operating modes:**
- **Interception mode**: The agent transparently intercepts incoming requests, extracts the TraT from the `Txn-Token` header, and verifies it before forwarding to your microservice. Requires the `tokenetes/service-port` annotation.
- **Delegation mode**: Requests are not intercepted. Your microservice explicitly calls the agent's local API at `POST http://localhost:9030/verify-trat` to verify TraTs.

steps.title: Write TraT resources for your APIs
steps.description:
Define Transaction Token (TraT) custom resources that describe the authorization context for each of your application's external APIs.

**1. Create a TraT resource for each API endpoint:**
```yaml
apiVersion: tokenetes.io/v1alpha1
kind: TraT
metadata:
  name: <trat-name>
  namespace: <your-namespace>
spec:
  path: "api/path/{#paramName}"        # API path with path variables
  method: "POST"                        # HTTP method
  purp: "<purpose>"                     # TraT purpose
  azdMapping:                           # Authorization details mapping
    fieldName:
      required: true
      value: "${body.field}"            # Sources: ${body.*}, ${header.*}, ${queryParameters.*}, ${variable_name}
  services:                             # Internal services that process this API
    - name: <service-name>
```

**2. Optionally create TraTExclusion resources to exclude endpoints (e.g., health checks):**
```yaml
apiVersion: tokenetes.io/v1alpha1
kind: TraTExclusion
metadata:
  name: <exclusion-name>
  namespace: <your-namespace>
spec:
  service: <service-name>
  endpoints:
    - path: "/health"
      method: "GET"
```

**3. Apply the resources:**
```bash
kubectl apply -f <trat-file>.yaml
kubectl apply -f <trat-exclusion-file>.yaml
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Confirm that all three Tokenetes components are running correctly.

**1. Check tconfigd:**
```bash
kubectl get pods -n tokenetes-system
```

**2. Check the Tokenetes service:**
```bash
kubectl get pods -n <your-namespace> | grep tokenetes
```

**3. Check agent sidecar injection (pods should show 2/2 containers):**
```bash
kubectl get pods -n <your-namespace>
```
Agent-injected pods will show `2/2` in the READY column, indicating both the application container and the Tokenetes agent sidecar are running.

**4. Verify SPIRE registrations:**
```bash
kubectl exec -n spire spire-server-0 -- \
    /opt/spire/bin/spire-server entry show
```
Confirm entries exist for tconfigd, the Tokenetes service, and each registered microservice.

steps.title: Verify TraT configuration
steps.description:
List and inspect the deployed Transaction Token (TraT) custom resources:
```bash
# List all TraTs in your namespace
kubectl get trats -n <your-namespace>

# Describe a specific TraT for details
kubectl describe trats/<trat-name> -n <your-namespace>

# List any TraT exclusions
kubectl get tratexclusions -n <your-namespace>
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation will show tconfigd running in the tokenetes-system namespace, the Tokenetes service pod running in your application namespace, and application pods with 2/2 containers (indicating agent sidecar injection). SPIRE entries should exist for tconfigd, Tokenetes, and all registered microservices. You can verify TraT configurations with `kubectl get trats -n <your-namespace>`.
codeSnippets:
git clone https://github.com/tokenetes/tconfigd.git
cd tconfigd/installation
# Edit config.yaml
./install.sh

kubectl apply -f kubernetes/tokenetes-service-account.yaml
kubectl apply -f kubernetes/tokenetes-deployment.yaml
kubectl apply -f kubernetes/tokenetes-service.yaml

kubectl get pods -n tokenetes-system
kubectl get pods -n <your-namespace>

uninstall.title: Remove agent sidecars from application pods
uninstall.description:
Remove the Tokenetes agent annotations from your deployments and restart them to remove the sidecar containers:
```bash
kubectl annotate deployment <your-service> -n <your-namespace> tokenetes/inject-sidecar- tokenetes/agent-mode- tokenetes/service-port-
kubectl rollout restart deployment <your-service> -n <your-namespace>
```
Optionally remove the namespace label:
```bash
kubectl label namespace <your-namespace> tokenetes-enabled-
```

uninstall.title: Delete Tokenetes resources
uninstall.description:
Remove the Tokenetes deployment, service, and service account from your namespace:
```bash
kubectl delete -f kubernetes/tokenetes-service.yaml
kubectl delete -f kubernetes/tokenetes-deployment.yaml
kubectl delete -f kubernetes/tokenetes-service-account.yaml
```

uninstall.title: Uninstall tconfigd
uninstall.description:
Remove tconfigd and its associated resources from the cluster:
```bash
cd tconfigd/installation
./uninstall.sh
```
If an uninstall script is not available, manually remove tconfigd resources:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace tokenetes-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and TraT resources
uninstall.description:
Remove any Transaction Token custom resources and CRDs that were created:
```bash
kubectl delete trats --all -n <your-namespace>
kubectl delete tratexclusions --all -n <your-namespace>
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep tokenetes | awk '{print $1}')
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
If you created a dedicated namespace for Tokenetes, remove it:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace <your-namespace>
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Pull the latest manifests
upgrade.description:
Update your local clones of the Tokenetes and tconfigd repositories to get the latest Kubernetes manifests:
```bash
cd tokenetes
git pull origin main
cd ../tconfigd
git pull origin main
```

upgrade.title: Re-apply the manifests
upgrade.description:
After updating the manifests with your environment-specific values, re-apply them:
```bash
kubectl apply -f kubernetes/tokenetes-service-account.yaml
kubectl apply -f kubernetes/tokenetes-deployment.yaml
kubectl apply -f kubernetes/tokenetes-service.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Restart agent-injected deployments
upgrade.description:
Restart your application deployments to pick up the latest agent sidecar image:
```bash
kubectl rollout restart deployment <your-service> -n <your-namespace>
```
Repeat for each deployment with agent injection enabled.

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Confirm the updated pods are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n tokenetes-system
kubectl get pods -n <your-namespace>
kubectl describe deployment tokenetes -n <your-namespace>
```

troubleshooting.title: tconfigd not running
troubleshooting.description:
Tokenetes requires tconfigd to be installed and running. Verify it is in the tokenetes-system namespace:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n tokenetes-system
kubectl logs <tconfigd-pod-name> -n tokenetes-system
```
If tconfigd is not running, check that SPIRE is configured correctly (k8sbundle plugin, ClusterRole permissions) and that tconfigd is registered with SPIRE. Reinstall from https://github.com/tokenetes/tconfigd if needed.

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Tokenetes pods are in CrashLoopBackOff, check the logs for configuration issues:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n <your-namespace>
```
Common causes include: missing or incorrect SPIRE agent socket path, tconfigd not running, incorrect trust domain configuration, or missing SPIRE registration entries.

troubleshooting.title: SPIRE agent socket not found
troubleshooting.description:
If logs show errors about the SPIRE agent socket, verify the hostPath in the deployment matches your SPIRE agent socket location:
```bash
kubectl describe deployment tokenetes -n <your-namespace>
```
The default path is `/run/spire/sockets`. Update the `spire-agent-socket` volume's `hostPath.path` in `tokenetes-deployment.yaml` if your setup differs.

troubleshooting.title: Agent sidecar not injected
troubleshooting.description:
If your application pods show `1/1` instead of `2/2` containers, the agent sidecar was not injected. Verify:
```bash
# Check namespace label
kubectl get namespace <your-namespace> --show-labels | grep tokenetes-enabled

# Check deployment annotations
kubectl get deployment <your-service> -n <your-namespace> -o jsonpath='{.spec.template.metadata.annotations}'
```
Ensure the namespace has `tokenetes-enabled: "true"` label and the deployment has `tokenetes/inject-sidecar: "true"` annotation. Restart the deployment after making changes.

troubleshooting.title: SPIRE registration errors
troubleshooting.description:
If components fail to obtain SVIDs (SPIFFE Verifiable Identity Documents), verify SPIRE registrations:
```bash
kubectl exec -n spire spire-server-0 -- \
    /opt/spire/bin/spire-server entry show
```
Ensure entries exist for tconfigd (`spiffe://[your-trust-domain]/tconfigd`), the Tokenetes service (`spiffe://[your-trust-domain]/tokenetes`), and each microservice. Check that selectors match the correct namespace and service account names.

troubleshooting.title: TraT resources not created
troubleshooting.description:
If `kubectl get trats` returns no resources, ensure tconfigd is running and the TraT CRD is installed:
```bash
kubectl get crd | grep tokenetes
kubectl get pods -n tokenetes-system
```
If the CRD is missing, tconfigd may not have been installed correctly.

prerequisites.description: You need a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher with SPIRE deployed for workload identity. The SPIRE server must be configured with the k8sbundle notifier plugin and have ClusterRole permissions for webhook configurations. kubectl and git must be installed on your local machine.
